# Questions in Livestream 1

Live Stream #1: The Debug Episode.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47</a> | What are your thoughts on Josephus' *Against Apion*? Is it good evidence for the exodus? | Josephus is useful for events near his own time, but he is not treated as a reliable witness for much older history. | He qualifies Josephus by chronology: Josephus can be useful close to his own period, but when he reaches back to much older events he depends on inherited legends and sources available in his day. For the exodus, Falk would want a stronger argument than Josephus alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=202" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22</a> | How did I come to know Christ? | He became a Christian at age seven while growing up in an evangelical Christian home. | He describes growing up with Christian parents and church life, then personally deciding at age seven to give his life to Christ. He stresses that it was quiet and personal rather than fear-driven or ceremonial. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=370" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:10</a> | Did you watch the Pharaohs' Golden Parade? If so, what was your reaction? | Yes. He thought it was a great spectacle and especially interesting as a modern ritual procession. | He enjoyed the event as spectacle and also read it through his work on ritual processions. Moving artifacts from the old museum to the GEM looked to him like a modern procession, interesting culturally even if he did not assign it special religious meaning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:26</a> | Have you looked into Ezekiel's prophecy concerning Nebuchadnezzar attacking Egypt? | Yes. He says Egypt was reduced to a rump state, though whether the prophecy is specifically about Nebuchadnezzar remains the debated question. | He says Egypt was eventually conquered, plundered, and reduced in power, so the broad historical direction fits. He connects Nebuchadnezzar with plundering Egyptian wealth, while noting that later Persian domination complicates exactly how the prophecy should be assigned. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=606" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:06</a> | Who are your favorite philosophical influences? | He studied philosophy of religion and philosophy of science, was especially interested in Plato, and says teachers influenced him more than any one modern writer. | He explains that he moved from physical sciences and biochemistry into philosophy, especially philosophy of religion and science. Plato interested him strongly, but he credits teachers more than a single modern author for shaping the tools he later used. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=796" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:16</a> | What are the highs and lows of being interviewed? | Highs include good conversations; lows are moments when his memory fails and he later realizes he got something wrong. | The high point is substantive conversation about ideas, evidence, and unexpected questions, which he prefers to formal debates. The low point is saying something from faulty memory and later realizing he misstated material he actually knows. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=902" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:02</a> | Can you tell a fun or weird story from Egyptian history that people do not usually see in basic history? | He points to the humorous story of Wenamun, a priest or scribe sent to Tyre for lumber whose trip becomes a comedy of errors. | He summarizes Wenamun as a priest or scribe sent to Tyre to obtain lumber for Amun's temple. After losing the gold and still needing to negotiate, the story becomes a humorous sequence of failed assumptions that also illuminates Egypt's relationship with the Levant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=995" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:35</a> | What is your favorite Egyptian creation myth? | He highlights the Memphite theology, where Ptah creates through thought and speech. | He mentions several Egyptian creation traditions, including Re emerging from an egg and Khnum shaping people on a wheel, but highlights the Memphite theology. In that account Ptah creates by thinking and speaking, which he compares with Genesis' emphasis on divine speech. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=1160" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:20</a> | What are your thoughts on the Levite hypothesis? | He is not persuaded by it and says there is not a good rationale for the hypothesis. | He says the Levite hypothesis depends heavily on a documentary-hypothesis framework. Since he thinks the Pentateuch preserves older Late Bronze Age vocabulary, names, and contextual evidence, he does not think that framework is needed, so the Levite-only exodus proposal loses force. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=1373" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:53</a> | If there is no evidence for King David's united monarchy, do you know a good scholar who talks about this? | He points to Eilat Mazar's work and says the question is now more about the monarchy's size and power than David's existence. | He points to Eilat Mazar's City of David work, the Tel Dan stele's house of David reference, and tenth-century Israelite material in the Shephelah. In his framing, the live scholarly question is now the size and power of the monarchy more than David's bare existence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=1526" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:26</a> | Is there any connection between the Book of the Dead and Genesis language about crawling on the belly or dust? | He does not see a connection; he says the Book of the Dead does not appear to play much role in biblical literature. | He distinguishes this from Egyptian texts where he does see stronger biblical parallels, such as the Hymn to the Aten or Instruction of Amenemope. For Genesis and the Book of the Dead, he says the parallels are weak and the Book of the Dead is a spell collection rather than the kind of Egyptian literature biblical writers seem to use. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=1622" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:02</a> | Why did many Egyptian dynasties practice sibling marriage? | He says most supposed sibling marriages were not literal; true consanguineous royal marriages were rare and tied to succession or royal blood ideology. | He says the premise is often overstated because spouses could call each other brother or sister as endearment. Actual close-kin royal marriages were rare and were tied to succession crises, royal blood ideology, or later Ptolemaic power-sharing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=1841" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:41</a> | Is Genesis 1-11 an ancient section of the Torah or a later Babylonian addition? | He rejects a later Babylonian addition and argues for origins in Late Bronze Age material, noting Egyptian vocabulary and lack of Old Iranian vocabulary. | He argues Genesis 1-11 reflects older Near Eastern and Egyptian conceptual worlds rather than a late Babylonian insertion. His reasons include Egyptian vocabulary across Genesis and Exodus, the absence of later Old Iranian vocabulary, and the way the text counters ancient mythic ideas for an exodus-era audience. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=2056" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:16</a> | With a late date for the exodus, which pharaoh would you place at the time of Joseph? | He places Joseph in the Hyksos period but says the specific king cannot be identified with current evidence. | He places Joseph broadly in the Hyksos period, beginning around the mid-second millennium BC, but says the Second Intermediate Period evidence is too fragmentary to name a king. Without an inscription or clearer record tying Joseph to a specific Hyksos ruler, he keeps the identification general. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=2232" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:12</a> | What are your thoughts on Tell el-Hammam being Sodom? | He says it is possible but the case is not strong enough yet and needs more work. | He allows Tell el-Hammam as possible, but not proven. He questions whether the occupation and destruction arguments have been shown strongly enough and says the excavator would need to do more to establish the Sodom identification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=2491" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:31</a> | What books would you recommend for an apologist making a case for the exodus? | He recommends recent background studies, *Did I Not Bring My People Out of Egypt?*, Kitchen's *On the Reliability of the Old Testament*, and his own work on the Ark. | He recommends using broad background studies and careful archaeological work rather than one proof text. He mentions *Did I Not Bring My People Out of Egypt?*, Kitchen's *On the Reliability of the Old Testament*, and his own Ark work as part of a cumulative historical case. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=2739" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:39</a> | Will scholarship on the exodus change over the next few decades as it has for the Davidic monarchy? | Yes, but he stresses careful handling of evidence and avoiding overstated claims. | He thinks the exodus debate may shift as the Davidic monarchy debate did, but only through careful scholarship. He warns against overplaying evidence or forcing weak parallels, using the Ipuwer Papyrus as an example of material that must be handled cautiously. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=2913" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:33</a> | When was Avaris abandoned, and how can we know the people who became Israelites lived there and left? | Avaris was abandoned in early to mid Dynasty 19; the Israelite connection is circumstantial because they would not yet have been called Israelites. | He dates Avaris' abandonment to early or mid Dynasty 19 and says its population was Semitic and Levantine in language, custom, and material culture. The link to later Israel is circumstantial because those people would not yet have carried the later national name Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=3139" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:19</a> | Did Hebrew exist as a distinct language at the time of the exodus, or was it an early proto-Canaanite dialect? | He says Hebrew likely existed as a distinct Western Semitic dialect already being spoken at Avaris. | He treats Hebrew as one Western Semitic dialect among related dialects, not as a language that appeared only after settlement. In his view, the Semitic population at Avaris could already have spoken a distinct dialect ancestral to Hebrew. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=3308" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:08</a> | When do you think the battle of Genesis 14 occurred if Amraphel of Shinar is Hammurabi? | The date remains uncertain; he places it sometime after Hammurabi's alliance with the Elamites and before Hammurabi later attacked them. | He keeps the date tentative and frames it relative to Hammurabi's political relationships. If Amraphel is Hammurabi, the Genesis 14 event would fit after cooperation with the Elamites but before Hammurabi later turned against them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=3506" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:26</a> | What do you think happened to Egypt after the exodus? | He argues Egypt was not defenseless, but lost much of its chariot force, made peace with the Hittites, and began losing its grip on Canaan. | He rejects the idea that Egypt became helpless, but says the loss of chariot forces would have weakened its strategic position. He connects this with peace with the Hittites and the gradual loss of Egyptian control in Canaan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=3818" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:38</a> | Is the "wings of the dawn" in Psalm 139:9 related to winged sun disk imagery like Hezekiah's seal? | Very possibly. He says Israelite texts and seals could adapt ancient Near Eastern divine symbols for Israelite purposes. | He thinks the connection is plausible because protective wing imagery was widely meaningful in the ancient Near East. Israelite texts and seals could repurpose imagery like winged disks or divine wings without adopting the original pagan theology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=4118" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:38</a> | If Yahu of Shasu is Yahweh, would that cause problems? | No. He cautions that Yahu may be a place or deity name, and says it would not create a theological problem either way. | He says the evidence is ambiguous because Yahu could refer to a place or to a deity. Even if it does refer to Yahweh, he does not see a theological problem, since the biblical story itself places Moses' encounter with Yahweh outside Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/uduERXxcb1E?t=4440" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:00</a> | Is there evidence of Israelites practicing monotheism or something similar in the Levant? | The quick answer is no; he says monotheism begins at Sinai but Israel took time to live consistently with it. | He answers that clear archaeological evidence for Israelite monotheism in the Levant is lacking. Theologically he locates monotheism at Sinai, but historically he says Israel struggled for a long time to live consistently with that commitment. |
